attack_surface.exposure: Source-generated __SnapshotParams() in LazyParamsGenerator captures every property's PrValue and FinalValue when a handler returns an error. The snapshot is attached to Error.Params and rendered by Error.cs FormatVerboseValue in stderr/log output.
attack_surface.trust_boundary: Handler boundary — any [Sensitive]-marked property carries cleartext (api keys, identity privates, settings secrets) at this point.
mitigations: Test report (test/report.cs) uses Json.DiagnosticOutput which masks [Sensitive] — but only at the typed-property level, not for ParamSnapshot.PrValue/FinalValue which are typed `object?`.
gaps: LazyParamsGenerator emits PrValue/FinalValue with no [Sensitive] check.
Error.cs FormatVerboseValue uses unfiltered JsonSerializer.Serialize for dict/list and value.ToString() for everything else — no mask.

attack_surface.exposure: Variables.Snapshot() (used by AssertionError) and Variables.GetAll() (used by Error verbose dump) return raw Data values keyed by name. No [Sensitive] filter at the dictionary-entry level.
attack_surface.trust_boundary: Dict/list entries are not properties — SensitivePropertyFilter (which acts on JsonTypeInfo properties) does not see them.
mitigations: test/report.cs uses Json.DiagnosticOutput, which masks [Sensitive] inside typed POCO values nested under the dict. So a typed Identity record with [Sensitive] PrivateKey *is* masked when nested.
But a top-level secret stored as a string (e.g. %apiKey% = 'sk-...') has no [Sensitive] hook — the var name doesn't carry the attribute.
gaps: Standing finding — confirmed unchanged on this branch.

attack_surface.exposure: Variables.Set on a dot/bracket path that targets a dict/list now JSON-roundtrips the source to break aliasing. On JsonException/NotSupportedException it falls back to the raw alias and only writes a debug line.
attack_surface.trust_boundary: Same-process aliasing — not a confidentiality boundary, but a known mutation-bug repro path.
mitigations: Debug-channel log line surfaces the failure when Debug is enabled.
gaps: Silent in production (Debug.IsEnabled false). The comment acknowledges 'alias risk' as an explicit choice.

attack_surface.exposure: TypeConverter.TryConvertTo deserializes any string into any non-primitive target type via System.Text.Json. Value source can be external (file/http/llm output). Target type bounded by handler parameter declarations.
attack_surface.trust_boundary: Action-parameter slot — schema-defined types only. STJ doesn't honor $type, so polymorphic gadgets are not directly triggerable.
mitigations: STJ refuses unknown polymorphism by default.
Capability properties (Context, Step, …) filtered from emit by Modules.Describe.
JsonIgnore on Path.Context, Step.Goal, etc. prevents cycles being injected.
gaps: ConvertTo recurses into nullable underlying types and list element types with no explicit depth guard. Bounded by source-string size in practice; not a usable attacker primitive but worth a depth guard.

attack_surface.exposure: DefaultBuilderProvider removed BuildingGuard from every action (Goals, GoalsSave, Validate, Merge, App, AppSave, PromoteGroups, EnrichResponse). Any signed .pr can now invoke `builder.goalsSave` at runtime, which writes a .pr file to disk via fs.File.WriteAllTextAsync.
attack_surface.trust_boundary: Signed .pr file. The user authorized the goal; the action surface widens what an authorized goal can do.
mitigations: File operations still go through IPLangFileSystem (the user-controlled sandboxed filesystem).
gaps: A signed third-party .goal that the user installed could now silently rebuild .pr artefacts at runtime, not just at build time. The prior guard was a privilege boundary between build mode and run mode; that boundary is gone (intentionally per commit 4633674c). Worth flagging so it is revisited.

attack_surface.exposure: New `formal` filter renders any value into the LLM's formal syntax. UnwrapFluid recurses through IFluidIndexable / IEnumerable.
attack_surface.trust_boundary: Template runs after Variables resolution — sees user variables. SSTI surface (already accepted risk per memory).
mitigations: UnwrapFluid only handles dicts (via key enumeration) and IEnumerable. Strings short-circuit, dicts short-circuit at non-IFluidIndexable.
gaps: UnwrapFluid has no recursion depth guard — a self-referential dict (key whose value points back to the parent) would StackOverflow. Hard to construct in practice (Fluid-wrapped dicts are built fresh from source data) but trivial to add a depth limit.

attack_surface.exposure: When Llm.* flags are enabled, OnAfterResponse / OnBeforeRequest write the full LLM system prompt, user message, schema, and raw response to either stderr or .build/traces/{traceId}/llm/{goalName}_{stepKey}.txt.
attack_surface.trust_boundary: User opt-in via --debug={"llm":{...}}. Debug.IsEnabled gates Write().
mitigations: Filename parts are sanitized via SanitizeFilenamePart using fs.Path.GetInvalidFileNameChars.
gaps: traceId from context.Trace.Id is NOT sanitized when included in the path. Today Trace.Id is internally generated (Guid) and safe; if a future code path lets external data set Trace.Id, this becomes a directory-traversal vector. Add belt-and-braces sanitization.

EmitLlmBlock writes raw blocks via AppendAllText — no [Sensitive] filter. By design (full untruncated content for debugging). Worth documenting as 'enabling llm.output=file produces files containing every byte sent to the LLM, including any settings/secrets the prompt resolved'.

attack_surface.exposure: Same as runtime2 — .pr files JSON-deserialize into Goal/Step/Action with case-insensitive matching.
attack_surface.trust_boundary: Cryptographic signature on the .pr is the boundary.

findings.vector: ParamSnapshot.PrValue / FinalValue are populated for every handler property, including those marked [Sensitive] (e.g. identity.PrivateKey, settings api-key parameters). On any handler error the source-generated ExecuteAsync attaches the snapshot to Error.Params. Error.cs:215,220 then renders both fields via FormatVerboseValue, which does NOT apply SensitivePropertyFilter — strings print verbatim, dicts/lists JSON-serialize without the mask. Error text reaches stderr, the debug channel, log files, and (depending on the consumer) trace artefacts.
findings.preconditions: A handler with at least one [Sensitive] parameter returns an error during execution. Standard error handling renders the formatted error string anywhere errors normally surface.
findings.impact: Cleartext secret (api key, private key, password) leaks into log output / error console / debug stream. Crosses confidentiality boundary.
findings.exploit_sketch: 1. Author a goal that calls a signing/identity/llm action with a [Sensitive] parameter. 2. Force the handler to error (e.g. malformed input). 3. Inspect the resulting error string in stderr or in the debug channel — PrValue/FinalValue line shows the secret in cleartext.
findings.proposed_fix: In LazyParamsGenerator's __SnapshotParams emit a [Sensitive]-aware mask: when the property has the attribute, set PrValue/FinalValue to the literal `"******"` (or null + a HasSensitive flag). Cheaper alternative: keep capture but make ParamSnapshot serialize through a [Sensitive]-aware filter and route Error.cs FormatVerboseValue for ParamSnapshot through Json.FormatForDiagnostic instead of the unfiltered path.

findings.vector: Variables.Snapshot() (used by AssertSnapshot.WithVariables) and Variables.GetAll() (used by Error verbose dump at Error.cs:230-250) return raw Dictionary<string, object?> values without [Sensitive] filtering. Top-level user variables holding secrets (e.g. %apiKey% = 'sk-...' loaded from settings) leak into AssertionError.Variables and into the verbose-error variable dump.
findings.preconditions: A goal stores a secret in a top-level variable (common pattern: load from settings, then pass to a handler). An assertion fails, OR the user runs with --debug={"verbose":true}.
findings.impact: Cleartext secret in test results.json (Json.DiagnosticOutput's mask only acts on typed-property metadata, not on dict-entry string values), in stderr, and in the error chain that bubbles through events.
findings.exploit_sketch: set %apiKey% = %settings.OpenAiKey%; (later) assert %something% equals %wrong%. results.json now contains 'apiKey': 'sk-...' under variables.
findings.proposed_fix: Add an out-of-band naming-convention filter on Variables.Snapshot/GetAll so vars matching a sensitivity pattern (e.g. starting with `_`, configured per app, or — better — when the Data carries a Sensitive flag) are masked. Long-term: extend Data.@this with a Sensitive flag honored by every snapshot/serialization path, not just typed properties.

findings.vector: Variables.Set's new dict/list deep-clone path does System.Text.Json.JsonSerializer.Serialize then Deserialize<object?>. Without depth limit on either pass, a deeply nested dict (cyclic or just very deep) can OOM during serialization or hit JsonException at depth 64 (default). The catch falls back to alias and only logs to Debug — when Debug is disabled the log line drops on the floor.
findings.preconditions: External-channel data lands in a typed parent's mutable dict/list slot, with depth >64 or with cycles.
findings.impact: Silent fallback to alias means the snapshot-clone safety the code claims to provide is bypassed. The acknowledged 'alias risk' line moves from debug-only-visible to invisible in production.
findings.exploit_sketch: POST a JSON object with 65 levels of nesting to an http endpoint that lands the body in a typed Step.Custom dict. JSON serialize fails on default MaxDepth, fallback aliases the source — subsequent set %src.x% = ... mutates the target.
findings.proposed_fix: On the JSON-clone path use Json.CamelCaseIndented options with explicit MaxDepth or, simpler, return the mutation-failure as a structured error instead of silently falling back. At minimum log to stderr (not just the debug channel) so the failure is visible in production.

findings.vector: FluidProvider.UnwrapFluid recurses through IFluidIndexable.Keys and IEnumerable items with no depth guard. A cycle in the underlying data graph would StackOverflow.
findings.preconditions: User-controlled dict/list value with self-reference reaches the `formal` filter via a template that calls it.
findings.exploit_sketch: Hard to construct in practice — Fluid wrappers are built fresh from source data each render. Mostly defense-in-depth.
findings.proposed_fix: Add `int depth = 0, int maxDepth = 32` to UnwrapFluid and bail to v.ToString() at the limit.

findings.vector: Debug.ResolveLlmFilePath builds the path .build/traces/{traceId}/llm/{safeGoal}_{stepKey}.txt. goalName and stepKey are sanitized via SanitizeFilenamePart, but traceId (from context.Trace.Id) is NOT sanitized. Today Trace.Id is internally generated (Guid) and safe.
findings.preconditions: A future code path (or test fixture) sets Trace.Id from external data — at that moment a `..` in Trace.Id traverses out of .build/traces.
findings.impact: Hypothetical today (Trace.Id is internal). Tripwire so a future regression doesn't slip through.
findings.exploit_sketch: If Trace.Id ever becomes settable from a user-supplied value: traceId='../../etc' would write llm/{goal}_{step}.txt outside the build dir.
findings.proposed_fix: Run traceId through SanitizeFilenamePart in ResolveLlmFilePath before Path.Combine.

findings.vector: DefaultBuilderProvider removed BuildingGuard from every builder action. builder.goalsSave / builder.appSave / builder.merge are now callable at runtime (not just build mode). A signed .goal can rebuild and overwrite arbitrary .pr files inside the user's app.
findings.preconditions: User installs a signed third-party .goal that calls a builder action at runtime.
findings.impact: A 'pr-rewrite' attack: the goal regenerates a security-relevant .pr at runtime, then resigns nothing (signature stays as the original installed signature, but content shifts). Whether the runtime re-verifies post-write determines if this is exploitable. Documented as intentional (commit 4633674c, BuildingGuardTests removed) so flagging for awareness, not as a bug.
findings.exploit_sketch: Goal calls builder.goalsSave on a target Goal it constructed in-memory, replacing .build/<target>.pr at runtime. If the runtime caches Goal objects from .pr load and doesn't re-verify, subsequent calls load attacker-chosen .pr content under the original signature.
findings.proposed_fix: Confirm with the architect that runtime invocation of builder.goalsSave is intentional. If yes — document the new threat model around .pr re-verification. If no — restore BuildingGuard or move .pr-mutating actions behind a [BuilderOnly] capability check.

summary: Branch posture is solid for the architectural changes (Catalog, TypeConverter, ParamSnapshot, Trace) — STJ defaults block direct deserialization gadgets, capability properties are filtered from the LLM catalog, file ops go through IPLangFileSystem. Two confidentiality issues stand out: (1) the new ParamSnapshot bypasses [Sensitive] when capturing PrValue/FinalValue and rendering them via FormatVerboseValue — same root cause as the standing Variables.Snapshot finding, both leak through error/log paths. (2) The standing GetAll/Snapshot finding remains uncloched. Three low-severity items are tripwires (UnwrapFluid recursion, JSON-clone fallback, traceId path component) worth fixing before they become real. The BuildingGuard removal is intentional but expands what a signed .pr can do at runtime — flagged for awareness.
